title: Sub-kelvin thermal conductivity of substrates and on-chip routing in quantum integrated systems
authors: Charles Bon-Mardion, Arnaud Lorin, Edouard Deschaseaux, Céline Feautrier, Daniel Mermin, Jean Charbonnier, Jing Li, Jean-Luc Sauvageot, Candice Thomas
abstract: The development of large-scale quantum systems increasingly relies on the close integration of heterogeneous components such as qubits, control electronics, and readout circuits, making thermal management at cryogenic temperatures a central challenge in such architectures. In this work, we present an experimental thermal study of two building blocks of such systems: the substrate and the on-chip routing. We first investigate the sub-kelvin thermal conductivity of four substrate materials: high-resistivity silicon, low-resistivity silicon, borosilicate, and sapphire. We report that high-resistivity silicon exhibits the highest thermal conductivity among the substrates studied ($5\cdot10^{-2}$~W/m$\cdot$K at 300~mK), while low-resistivity silicon, borosilicate, and sapphire show lower values ($8\cdot10^{-4}$~W/m$\cdot$K, 2$\cdot10^{-3}$~W/m$\cdot$K, and 2$\cdot10^{-3}$~W/m$\cdot$K at 300~mK, respectively). Ballistic conductance evaluation using a finite-element non-equilibrium Green's function approach further allows us to extract the phonon mean free path in each substrate and gives insights into the involved scattering mechanisms. Additionally, we employ a dedicated test vehicle to evaluate the impact of on-chip routing on the thermal conductance of the system. Our measurements with superconducting Nb routing lines reveal that the routing increases the in-plane thermal conductance of the system, but the substrate remains the dominant heat path. These results highlight the critical role of the substrate choice within quantum systems and underscore the importance of function partitioning through 3D integration approaches for more efficient thermal management in quantum architectures.
summary: This paper provides experimental measurements of the thermal conductivity of various substrates at sub-Kelvin temperatures. It identifies high-resistivity silicon as the best candidate for heat removal and highlights the need for 3D integration to manage heat in large-scale quantum processors.
main_problem: Thermal management is a critical bottleneck for scaling large-scale quantum integrated systems, as heat from control electronics can cause qubit decoherence.
main_result: High-resistivity silicon exhibits the highest thermal conductivity among studied substrates, while Nb routing lines increase in-plane conductance but the substrate remains the dominant heat path.
method: Thermal conductivity was measured using a heater and RuO2 thermometer in the sub-Kelvin regime, with analysis using a finite-element non-equilibrium Green's function approach to extract phonon mean free paths.
model_or_system: The study investigates various substrates (high-resistivity and low-resistivity silicon, sapphire, and borosilicate glass) and an on-chip routing test vehicle featuring superconducting Niobium lines on silicon.
key_observables: Thermal conductivity (kappa), phonon mean free path (MFP), and in-plane thermal conductance.
important_parameters: Sub-Kelvin temperature regime (50 mK to 1.2 K), power dissipation in the nanowatt to microwatt range, and phonon mean free path ranging from 30 to 4000 micrometers.
main_assumptions: The thermal resistance of NbTi measurement wires and the volume of Stycast 1266 are assumed to be negligible; self-heating of the thermometer is assumed to be negligible at 1 nW.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the measurement setup and thermal model; Fig 5 and 6 illustrate the routing test vehicle setup and temperature gradients as a function of power; Supplementary figures detail the measurement validation and packaging workflow.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the thermal management challenge, presents experimental characterization of substrate thermal conductivity, evaluates the impact of on-chip Nb routing, and concludes with implications for 3D quantum architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Experimental setup for the thermal conductivity study of various substrates. (b) Simplified equivalent thermal model of the setup.

FIG. 4. (a) Comparison of simulated ballistic conductance per area (continuous lines), G0/A, with experimental measured conductance per area, G/A. (b) By using Eq. 3, the mean free paths are extracted as function of temperature for various substrates. Horizontal dashed line is the Casimir boundary-limited mean free path obtained with Eq. 4. The dash-dotted black line is the mean free path of phonon limited by hole-phonon scattering for the LR silicon substrate. Dotted lines are guides for T−1 (red) and T−1.5 (black) behavior.
FIG. 5. (a) Experimental setup for the thermal conductance study of an on-chip routing test vehicle. (b) Simplified thermal equivalent model.

FIG. 6. TL and TR thermometer temperatures as a function of the applied power Q for two cryostat base temperatures: (a) at T0 = 50 mK and (b) at T0 = 300 mK. For both measurement runs, a thermal gradient is established between TR and TL.

FIG. 7. (a) Obtained temperature difference between the right TR and left TL thermometer versus the applied heat power Q. (b) Calculated thermal conductance G = Q/∆T as a function of the mean temperature (TR +TL)/2 for the two measurement runs. The continuous line is the expected conduction from the LR silicon substrate only. The thickness used for the calculation was 250 µm, corresponding to the edge of the device, outside of the backside cavity. Dashed line is a T 2 guide for the eyes.
